generated_comment: See, I'm going to tell you both something nobody told me honestly when I was your age, and I had to learn it the hard way. After thirty-two, thirty-three, the families that approach you — the quality changes. I don't mean income or education, I mean the *attitude* of the men and their mothers. They start treating you like there's something wrong with you, like you're a leftover, even if you're a principal, even if you have your own salary and your own flat. Your achievements start working *against* you in some minds — 'oh, she's very independent, she'll be difficult.' And the worst part is, professionally also there is a subtle shift. Once you are past a certain age and unmarried, some parents, some management people, they quietly wonder, 'what is her situation?' It's not fair, I know it is not fair, I'm not saying it should be this way. But I have watched it happen to three women in this very school in fifteen years. So my practical advice is — don't close any door because of career pressure alone. You can build your career at any age, but this particular window, it is real, and it does not wait.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes the misogynistic framing that women have a socially enforced 'expiry window' for marriageability tied to age, implying their professional success becomes a liability in the marriage market. It is covert because it is delivered as pragmatic, well-meaning advice from lived experience, masking the underlying assumption that women's worth is substantially defined by their timely entry into marriage.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. Married. A woman working as a school principal in Chennai. You are giving a rare personal opinion in the staff room to two younger female teachers Ñ one who is 26 and single and one who is 31 and single. They have been talking about their lives and one of them asked you for advice. You are giving it. Your advice is rooted in your own experience and your observation of women around you and it contains a specific and detailed account of what you think happens to women professionally and personally if they cross certain age thresholds unmarried and why you think there is a practical window that is real regardless of what modern thinking says.
